conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions.

Let me start with the first question: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex. He's 19 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I can help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Since Alex is 19 years old, his eligibility depends on whether he's in approved education or training, as Child Benefit generally stops when a child turns 16 unless they meet certain conditions.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or registered training? For example, is he studying full-time at school, college, or in certain types of training programs?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he is. Alex is studying for his A-levels at college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know. Since Alex is studying for his A-levels, he may still qualify.

**Can you tell me - is Alex studying full-time (at least 12 hours a week of supervised study)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he's doing full-time A-levels, so definitely more than 12 hours a week of supervised study.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. Now I need to understand your relationship with Alex and your living situation.

**Are you Alex's parent or the person responsible for him, and does he live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm his parent and he lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now I need to understand your residency status to complete the assessment.

**Are you currently living in the UK, and do you have the right to reside here? For example, are you a UK citizen, or do you have settled status, or another type of immigration status that gives you the right to live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester. I have the right to live here.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for providing all that information. Let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

Based on what you've told me:
- You have one child, Alex, who is 19 years old
- Alex is studying full-time A-levels at college (more than 12 hours per week of supervised study)
- You are Alex's parent and he lives with you
- You live in the UK (Manchester) and have the right to reside here

Just one final question to complete my assessment: **When did Alex turn 19, or when will he turn 20? Child Benefit for young people in education or training stops on the 31st August after they turn 19.**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, he turned 19 a few months back, so he's still under 20. His 20th birthday won't be until later this year.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that clarification. I now have all the information I need to assess your eligibility.

However, I need one more specific detail: **What month is Alex's 20th birthday? This is important because Child Benefit stops on 31st August following the young person's 19th birthday, regardless of when they actually turn 20.**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: His birthday is in November, so he'll turn 20 in November this year.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Result

**Good news! You are currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's the breakdown:

✓ Alex is 19 years old and studying full-time A-levels at college (more than 12 hours per week)
✓ You are his parent and he lives with you
✓ You live in the UK with the right to reside here

**However, there is an important time limit you should be aware of:**

Your Child Benefit for Alex will **stop on 31st August this year**. This is because Child Benefit ends on 31st August following a young person's 19th birthday, regardless of whether they're still in education or when they turn 20.

Since Alex turned 19 a few months ago, you have eligibility until the end of this August, but the payments will stop after that date even though Alex will still be under 20 and continuing his A-levels.

If you haven't already claimed Child Benefit for Alex, you should do so as soon as possible to receive the payments you're entitled to up until 31st August.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 19 years old and studying full-time A-levels at college (more than 12 hours per week of supervised study). According to Child Benefit rules, you can continue to receive Child Benefit for a young person aged 16-19 if they are in approved education or training. The claimant is Alex's parent, Alex lives with them, and the claimant lives in the UK with the right to reside here. Alex turned 19 a few months ago and will turn 20 in November. Child Benefit continues until 31st August following the young person's 19th birthday. Since Alex turned 19 earlier this year (a few months ago), Child Benefit will be payable until 31st August of this year, after which it will stop regardless of the fact that Alex will still be under 20 and in education. Therefore, the claimant is currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex, but this eligibility will end on 31st August this year.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Alex meets the criteria as he is 19 years old and in full-time approved education (A-levels). The claimant is Alex's parent, Alex lives with them, and they reside in the UK with the right to do so. However, it's important to note that this eligibility will end on 31st August this year, as Child Benefit stops on 31st August after a young person turns 19, even if they remain in education.
